notes: searches: ~10 web + local greps (Wylie vol3, Deanesly, D'Aubigne). clusters: 7 (Tyndale & Bible-printing; medieval vernacular Bible access [escalation]; Latimer/English reformers; Scotland/Celtic church; English dissenters/Bunyan/Baxter; Wesley/Methodism; Antinomianism). Delta form used (157 claims with very long frozen quotes exceed single-write budget; merge via scripts/merge_stage.py). Primary chapter source identified as J.A. Wylie History of Protestantism vol.3 (Tyndale 'eagles' and 'plough boy' quotes located verbatim there); reformer quotes trace to Wylie/Foxe/D'Aubigne and Wesley's Journal/Works. Control sample of 3 needs_verification:false claims: GC-14-053, GC-14-088, GC-14-089 (all confirmed genuinely interpretive; embedded empirical elements noted). Medieval-Bible-access cluster (002-004,006,009) carries source_quality note; academic source = Deanesly (Cambridge UP 1920). Low-confidence items: 0.

GC-14-001.rationale: Theological claim (divine impulse behind Tyndale's work); not empirically testable.

GC-14-002.rationale: [medieval vernacular Bible access] Wycliffe's English Bible was translated from the Latin Vulgate, which contained textual errors relative to the Greek/Hebrew. source_quality: academic-full (Deanesly).
GC-14-002.modern_consensus: Wycliffite Bible rendered from the Vulgate.

sources.title: M. Deanesly, The Lollard Bible (Cambridge UP, 1920), local copy
sources.note: 'The Holy Bible: made from the Latin Vulgate by John Wyclif and his followers'

GC-14-003.rationale: [medieval vernacular Bible access] Wycliffite Bible was pre-print (manuscript only) and costly; Deanesly documents ownership concentrated among clergy, gentry, nuns and the highest classes by license. source_quality: academic-full.
GC-14-003.modern_consensus: Manuscript-only, expensive; owners mostly well-off, though ~250 mss survive (wider than once assumed).

sources.title: M. Deanesly, The Lollard Bible (Cambridge UP, 1920), local copy
sources.note: ownership of English Bibles restricted to highest classes by individual license

GC-14-004.rationale: [medieval vernacular Bible access] The 1407/1408 Constitutions of Oxford (Arundel) banned unauthorized English Scripture, restricting circulation. source_quality: academic-full.
GC-14-004.modern_consensus: Arundel's Constitutions of 1408 proscribed unlicensed vernacular translations.

sources.title: M. Deanesly, The Lollard Bible (Cambridge UP, 1920), local copy

GC-14-005.rationale: [common_knowledge] Erasmus published his Greek/Latin NT in 1516; Luther's theses 1517.

GC-14-006.rationale: [medieval vernacular Bible access] Erasmus's 1516 Novum Instrumentum was the first PUBLISHED Greek NT; the Complutensian Polyglot was printed 1514 but not released until 1522, so GC's 'first printed' is slightly imprecise while substantially correct. source_quality: academic-partial/encyclopedic.
GC-14-006.modern_consensus: Erasmus's was the first published Greek NT; Complutensian printed earlier but published later.

sources.title: Bridwell Library, 'The First Published New Testament in Greek'
sources.note: Complutensian printed 1514, released 1522; Erasmus 1516 first published

GC-14-007.rationale: Erasmus's edition corrected Vulgate errors and supplied a fresh Latin rendering with critical notes; standard scholarship.

GC-14-008.rationale: Erasmus's Greek NT circulated widely among the learned and stimulated reform; standard historiography.

sources.title: Novum Instrumentum omne - Wikipedia
sources.note: wide distribution among educated; impetus to reform

GC-14-009.rationale: [medieval vernacular Bible access] After 1516 the common English reader still lacked a printed vernacular Bible (Greek/Latin editions were for scholars); no printed English NT until Tyndale 1526. source_quality: academic-full context (Deanesly).

sources.title: M. Deanesly, The Lollard Bible (Cambridge UP, 1920), local copy

GC-14-010.rationale: Interpretive framing (Tyndale 'completed' Wycliffe's work); not a discrete checkable fact.

GC-14-011.rationale: Tyndale studied Erasmus's Greek NT; Wylie records he 'kept his Greek New Testament ever beside him.'

sources.title: J.A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, vol. 3 (local copy)

GC-14-012.rationale: Tyndale urged doctrines be tested by Scripture; consistent with Wylie and biographies.

sources.title: J.A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, vol. 3 (local copy)

GC-14-013.rationale: The 'eagles' exchange is located verbatim (abridged) in Wylie vol.3 ('Do you know who taught the eagles to find their prey?...it is you who have hidden them from us'); White's version, likely via D'Aubigne, extends it slightly but faithfully transmits a period-attested saying.

sources.title: J.A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, vol. 3 (local copy)

GC-14-014.rationale: Tyndale's preaching drew interest and converts; Wylie describes his preaching tours (Bristol, St Austin's Green).

sources.title: J.A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, vol. 3 (local copy)

GC-14-015.rationale: Wylie records priests destroyed Tyndale's work after he left ('no sooner had he sowed the seed than the priests hastened to destroy it...the field was ravaged').

sources.title: J.A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, vol. 3 (local copy)

GC-14-016.rationale: Matches Wylie's Tyndale saying ('if the people of England had the Word of God in their own language this would not happen... Without this it will be impossible to establish the laity in the truth'); White expands rhetorically but faithfully.

sources.title: J.A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, vol. 3 (local copy)

GC-14-017.rationale: Tyndale's vernacular-Scripture argument (temple songs in Hebrew, epistles in addressees' tongue) matches Wylie's report; period-attested reasoning.

sources.title: J.A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, vol. 3 (local copy)

GC-14-018.rationale: Narrator's bridging sentence; the substance (church doctors disagreeing) is a commonplace of Reformation polemic and matches the disputants Wylie describes at Sodbury.

GC-14-019.rationale: Theological proposition (only the Bible yields truth); not empirically testable.

GC-14-020.rationale: Tyndale's 'one holdeth this doctor, another that... verily by God's word' argument is a period-attested saying transmitted via White's sources (Wylie/D'Aubigne).

sources.title: J.A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, vol. 3 (local copy)

GC-14-021.rationale: The Catholic doctor's 'We were better to be without God's laws than the pope's' is recorded in Foxe's Acts and Monuments as part of the plough-boy exchange; well attested.

sources.title: Foxe's Acts and Monuments (as cited in Tyndale scholarship)

GC-14-022.rationale: The plough-boy vow is located in Wylie vol.3 verbatim ('cause the boy that driveth the plough to know more of the Scriptures than the priests do'), sourced there to Foxe; one of the best-attested Tyndale sayings.

sources.title: J.A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, vol. 3 (local copy)

GC-14-023.rationale: Tyndale's resolve to translate the NT is documented; Wylie: 'the sublime idea entered his mind of translating and printing the Scriptures.'

sources.title: J.A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, vol. 3 (local copy)

GC-14-024.rationale: Tyndale went to London (1523) seeking Tunstall's patronage and worked there for a time (under Humphrey Monmouth); Wylie confirms he 'repaired (1523) to the metropolis.'

sources.title: J.A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, vol. 3 (local copy)

GC-14-025.rationale: Finding no support in England, Tyndale left for the Continent (1524); standard biography.

GC-14-026.rationale: Tyndale began printing the English NT on the Continent (Cologne, then Worms).

GC-14-027.rationale: Printing was interrupted at Cologne (1525, raided) before completion at Worms; 'twice stopped / moved city' matches the Cologne-to-Worms sequence.

GC-14-028.rationale: [common_knowledge] Tyndale completed the NT at Worms; Luther defended the gospel at the Diet of Worms (1521).

GC-14-029.rationale: Worms was sympathetic to the Reformation and Tyndale finished printing there unhindered; standard account.

GC-14-030.rationale: ~3,000 copies of the Worms 1526 NT is the standard figure (estimates 3,000-6,000). Further 1526-27 (pirated Antwerp) reprints followed, consistent with 'another edition the same year,' though this second-edition detail traces to White's sources.
GC-14-030.modern_consensus: Worms octavo 1526, print run ~3,000 (some say 6,000); pirate reprints soon after.

GC-14-031.rationale: Copies were smuggled into England despite port searches and circulated widely; standard history.

sources.title: The Life and Work of William Tyndale

GC-14-032.rationale: The prelate who bought up Tyndale's Testaments (via Augustine Packington, c.1529) was Cuthbert Tunstall as Bishop of LONDON; he was translated to Durham only in 1530. GC (following its sources) calls him 'bishop of Durham.' The anecdote's substance is accurate; only the episcopal title is wrong. Low-severity title error; defender to check whether White's immediate source made the same slip.
GC-14-032.modern_consensus: Tunstall, Bishop of London (1522-1530), arranged the buy-up before his 1530 translation to Durham.

sources.title: Tyndale Society Journal 17 — A Tale of Two Packingtons
sources.note: buy-up c.1529, while Tunstall was Bishop of London

GC-14-033.rationale: The money from the buy-up funded a new/revised edition; recorded in Halle's Chronicle and Foxe. (Bishop's title Durham vs London noted at GC-14-032.)

sources.title: The Day an English Bishop Burned Bibles (recounting Halle/Foxe)

GC-14-034.rationale: Imprisoned at Vilvoorde, Tyndale was pressed to name his backers; the incentive-to-recant framing is consistent with Foxe's account of his captivity.

GC-14-035.rationale: Tyndale's witty reply that the bishop had helped him most (by overpaying for the leftover stock) is the standard Foxe/Halle anecdote. (Title should be Bishop of London per GC-14-032.)

sources.title: The Day an English Bishop Burned Bibles

GC-14-036.rationale: Tyndale was imprisoned ~16 months (c.500 days) at Vilvoorde Castle, 1535-1536.

GC-14-037.rationale: [common_knowledge] Tyndale was executed (strangled and burned) as a martyr in 1536.

GC-14-038.rationale: Interpretive/figurative (his 'weapons' aiding others through the centuries); not a discrete fact.

GC-14-039.rationale: Latimer preached for vernacular Scripture; consistent with his sermons and Foxe.

GC-14-040.rationale: The quoted sentiments (God as Author of Scripture; rulers bound to obey His word; be directed by God's word not forefathers) are drawn from Latimer's sermons; faithfully transmitted period quotation.

GC-14-041.rationale: Robert Barnes and John Frith were early English reformers and associates of Tyndale.

sources.title: The Life and Work of William Tyndale

GC-14-042.rationale: Nicholas Ridley and Thomas Cranmer were later leaders of the English Reformation. 'The Ridleys' (plural) is loose but Nicholas Ridley is the principal referent.

GC-14-043.rationale: Cranmer, Ridley, Barnes and others were university-trained clergy formerly in good standing in the Roman church; standard biography.

GC-14-044.rationale: Causal-interpretive but well grounded: these men's insider knowledge of the church shaped their critique; a defensible characterization.

GC-14-045.rationale: Interpretive/figurative ('mysteries of Babylon'); not empirically testable.

GC-14-046.rationale: The 'most diligent prelate in all England... the devil' passage is from Latimer's Sermon of the Plough (1548), verbatim in substance; well attested.

GC-14-047.rationale: Interpretive thesis linking the reformers to the Waldenses/Wycliffe/Huss/Luther/Zwingli by a shared 'sola scriptura' principle; a theological-historical construal, not a single checkable fact.

GC-14-048.rationale: Interpretive characterization of the reformers' stance on conscience; not a discrete testable proposition.

GC-14-049.rationale: Latimer's 'we shall this day light such a candle... as I trust shall never be put out,' spoken to Ridley at their burning (Oxford, 1555), is recorded in Foxe's Acts and Monuments; among the most famous martyr sayings.

sources.title: Hugh Latimer - Wikiquote (Foxe, Acts and Monuments)

GC-14-050.rationale: White follows Wylie's Culdee/Celtic-church thesis, in which Columba's Iona mission planted a purer gospel that survived as a distinct witness. Modern scholarship treats the early Scottish (Celtic) church as fully catholic in doctrine, distinctive only in customs (Easter dating, tonsure), not a proto-Protestant remnant.
GC-14-050.modern_consensus: The Celtic/Scottish church was doctrinally catholic; the 'proto-Protestant Culdee remnant' reading is rejected.
GC-14-050.period_historiography: Wylie vol.3 (chapter on 'Suppression of the Culdees') frames Columba's Iona as kindling a torch of independent gospel truth.

sources.title: J.A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, vol. 3 (local copy)
David I of Scotland - Wikipedia
sources.note: Scottish church catholic; brought to Roman conformity 11th-12th c

GC-14-051.rationale: 'For hundreds of years after the churches of England submitted to Rome, those of Scotland maintained their freedom' reflects Wylie's Culdee-independence narrative. Modern historians: the Celtic north retained distinctive customs after the Synod of Whitby (664) but conformed on Easter by the early 8th c. and was never outside catholic communion; 'freedom from Rome' is anachronistic period framing.
GC-14-051.modern_consensus: Distinctive Celtic customs, not doctrinal independence from Rome; conformity progressive from the 8th c.
GC-14-051.period_historiography: Wylie vol.3 portrays Scotland as retaining independence until forcible Romanization.

sources.title: J.A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, vol. 3 (local copy)
Saint Margaret of Scotland - Wikipedia
sources.note: Margaret (1070-93) brought Celtic church into Roman conformity

GC-14-052.rationale: The Roman reorganization of the Scottish church did crystallize in the 12th century under David I (r.1124-53), who rebuilt dioceses/parishes on the Roman pattern; papal-protected status via Cum universi (1192). GC's 12th-century dating is broadly correct.
GC-14-052.modern_consensus: David I Romanized the Scottish church in the 12th c.; Cum universi 1192.

GC-14-053.rationale: CONTROL SAMPLE (extractor flagged needs_verification:false). Verified: the sentence is a rhetorical superlative ('in no country did it exercise a more absolute sway. Nowhere was the darkness deeper'). It smuggles an unfalsifiable comparative claim (Scotland had the most absolute papal sway of any country), which cannot be tested. Extractor's non-flag is defensible; genuinely interpretive.

GC-14-054.rationale: Lollardy did reach Scotland with Wycliffite ideas; documented martyrs include James Resby (1407), Paul Crawar (1433) and the Lollards of Kyle (c.1494). 'Every century had its witnesses' is a reasonable summary via Wylie.

sources.title: J.A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, vol. 3 (local copy)

GC-14-055.rationale: Luther's writings and Tyndale's NT were smuggled into Scotland in the 1520s (Patrick Hamilton had studied Lutheran doctrine on the Continent); standard history.

sources.title: John Knox and the Scottish Reformation - Historic UK

GC-14-056.rationale: The internal chronology is consistent (12th-c Romanization to mid-16th-c Reformation ~ four centuries); Reformation ideas did spread quietly before open reform. The 'oppression' framing is interpretive but the timeframe checks out.

GC-14-057.rationale: Scottish martyrdoms (Hamilton, Wishart) preceded and energized the Reformation movement; standard historiography.

sources.title: John Knox and the Scottish Reformation - Historic UK

GC-14-058.rationale: Roman Catholic authorities in Scotland (e.g. Cardinal Beaton) executed prominent reform figures; consistent with the record.

sources.title: John Knox and the Scottish Reformation - Historic UK

GC-14-059.rationale: Patrick Hamilton (burned 1528, of noble/royal-connected birth) and George Wishart (burned 1546) were Scottish martyrs, with lesser-known followers also executed.

GC-14-060.rationale: Theological framing (Knox raised up 'under God' to end popery); the providential claim is not testable. Knox's historical association with Wishart is treated at GC-14-062.

GC-14-061.rationale: Knox turned from Roman tradition to Scripture; standard biography.

GC-14-062.rationale: Knox was closely associated with Wishart (acting as a bodyguard before Wishart's 1546 arrest); Wishart's influence confirmed his commitment to reform.

GC-14-063.rationale: Knox was famously reluctant to accept the preaching call at St Andrews (1547), consenting only after inner struggle; recorded in his own History.

GC-14-064.rationale: Interpretive characterization of Knox's lifelong resolve; not a discrete fact.

GC-14-065.rationale: Knox had several confrontational interviews with Mary, Queen of Scots (1561-63) and maintained his stance; documented in his History of the Reformation in Scotland.

GC-14-066.rationale: Mary charged Knox with teaching subjects to receive a religion the state forbade and thus with sedition; recorded in Knox's History.

sources.title: The History of the Reformation of Religion in Scotland (Knox), Gutenberg

GC-14-067.rationale: Knox's argument that true religion derives from God not princes, with the Abraham-under-Pharaoh and apostles-under-Rome examples, is from his reported dialogue with Mary in his own History; faithfully transmitted.

sources.title: The History of the Reformation... in Scotland (Knox), Gutenberg

GC-14-068.rationale: Mary's question 'whom shall I believe, and who shall be judge?' is from Knox's recorded dialogue in his History.

sources.title: The History of the Reformation... in Scotland (Knox), Gutenberg

GC-14-069.rationale: Knox's reply ('believe God, that plainly speaketh in His word'; Scripture self-interpreting) is from his History; faithfully transmitted period quotation.

sources.title: The History of the Reformation... in Scotland (Knox), Gutenberg

GC-14-070.rationale: Interpretive framing ('at the peril of his life... in the ear of royalty'); rhetorical, not a discrete fact.

GC-14-071.rationale: Knox persisted until Scotland's Reformation Parliament (1560) abolished papal jurisdiction; standard history.

GC-14-072.rationale: Establishing Protestantism as the national religion reduced but did not end religious persecution (dissenters continued to suffer).

GC-14-073.rationale: The Church of England renounced many Roman doctrines while retaining much liturgical form; standard characterization.

GC-14-074.rationale: The Act of Supremacy (1534/1559) rejected papal supremacy and made the monarch Supreme Head/Governor of the church.

GC-14-075.rationale: Acts of Uniformity required conformity to the established church; dissenters were penalized for well over a century.

GC-14-076.rationale: The Great Ejection (1662) removed ~2,000 ministers who refused conformity; 'thousands' is within the range of estimates (1,800-2,500).

GC-14-077.rationale: The Conventicle Acts (1664, 1670) and Five Mile Act (1665) forbade unauthorized religious meetings under penalty of fines, imprisonment and transportation.

GC-14-078.rationale: Persecuted dissenters met covertly (private houses, out-of-the-way places, night gatherings); consistent with the Conventicle-era record.

GC-14-079.rationale: Many dissenters were fined, jailed and some transported/banished; broadly accurate for the Restoration persecution.

GC-14-080.rationale: Persecuted English dissenters (Puritans, Pilgrims, Quakers) emigrated to America and contributed to traditions of civil/religious liberty; standard history, though the causal simplification is White's framing.

GC-14-081.rationale: John Bunyan wrote The Pilgrim's Progress during imprisonment (Bedford, 1660s-70s) for unlicensed preaching; standard biography.

GC-14-082.rationale: The Pilgrim's Progress was published 1678; 'over two hundred years' before the 1911 GC is arithmetically correct (~233 years).

GC-14-083.rationale: Bunyan wrote both The Pilgrim's Progress (1678) and Grace Abounding to the Chief of Sinners (1666).

GC-14-084.rationale: Richard Baxter, John Flavel and Joseph Alleine were prominent ejected/nonconformist ministers and authors of the period.

GC-14-085.rationale: John Flavel wrote The Fountain of Life (1673) and The Method of Grace (1681).

GC-14-086.rationale: Richard Baxter wrote The Reformed Pastor (1656) and The Saints' Everlasting Rest (1650).

GC-14-087.rationale: Whitefield and the Wesleys led the Evangelical Revival from c.1738. 'A hundred years later' than the Baxter/Flavel/Alleine era (active c.1650s-1680s) is a loose rounding (~55-80 years); rhetorical rather than precise.
GC-14-087.modern_consensus: Evangelical Revival began late 1730s, ~75 years after the Great Ejection.

GC-14-088.rationale: CONTROL SAMPLE (needs_verification:false). Verified: primarily an evaluative characterization ('religious declension hardly to be distinguished from heathenism'). It embeds a contestable empirical claim about the pre-revival state of English religion; modern historians debate the 'spiritual darkness before the revival' trope as Evangelical polemic. Extractor's non-flag defensible; core is interpretive.

GC-14-089.rationale: CONTROL SAMPLE (needs_verification:false). Verified: chiefly evaluative. Embedded factual element (natural religion/Deism prominent in early-18th-c clergy theology) is broadly accurate, but the sweeping moral condemnation of the classes is polemical characterization, not a checkable fact. Extractor's non-flag defensible.

GC-14-090.rationale: By the early 18th c. the Reformation emphasis on justification by faith had faded in much English preaching in favor of moralism/works; a defensible period-Evangelical characterization (though 'Romish principle' is polemical).

GC-14-091.rationale: Whitefield and the Wesleys were Church of England members who initially pursued salvation through disciplined virtue/ordinances (the Oxford 'Holy Club'); standard biography.

GC-14-092.rationale: Charles Wesley recounted a serious illness during which he reflected on the ground of his hope; consistent with Methodist biography.

sources.title: 1738 John & Charles Wesley Experience Conversions - Christian History

GC-14-093.rationale: Charles Wesley's 'I have used my best endeavors to serve God' and his reflection on having 'nothing else to trust to' are drawn from Methodist biographical sources; faithfully transmitted.

sources.title: The Conversion of the Wesley Brothers - Church History Review

GC-14-094.rationale: Wesley taught that true religion is inward and that God's law reaches the thoughts; central to his theology.

GC-14-095.rationale: The Oxford Holy Club practiced rigorous self-denial, charity and religious exactness in pursuit of holiness; standard biography.

GC-14-096.rationale: Wesley by his own account did not attain assurance/freedom from sin's condemnation through these efforts before 1738; documented in his Journal.

sources.title: John Wesley and Faith at Aldersgate - The Gospel Coalition

GC-14-097.rationale: The parallel to Luther's monastic struggle is apt: Luther was an Augustinian friar (Erfurt, 1505-08) tormented by 'how shall man be just before God?' The comparison is interpretive but the Luther facts are correct.

sources.title: John Wesley and Faith at Aldersgate - The Gospel Coalition

GC-14-098.rationale: Theological/figurative ('fires of divine truth... ancient torch'); not empirically testable.

GC-14-099.rationale: After the Bohemian defeat (White Mountain, 1620), Protestantism was suppressed; refugees preserved the faith, some later gathering at Herrnhut in Saxony (the renewed Moravian Church).

sources.title: The Moravians and John Wesley - Christian History

GC-14-100.rationale: The Moravians who influenced Wesley descended from the Bohemian Brethren who took refuge in Saxony (Herrnhut).

sources.title: The Moravians and John Wesley - Christian History

GC-14-101.rationale: John and Charles Wesley, ordained CoE clergy, went on the Georgia mission (1735-36).

GC-14-102.rationale: A company of Moravians was aboard the ship (the Simmonds) with the Wesleys; documented in Wesley's Journal.

GC-14-103.rationale: Violent Atlantic storms occurred and Wesley confessed his lack of assurance facing death; from his Journal.

GC-14-104.rationale: Wesley contrasted the Moravians' calm trust with his own fear; from his Journal.

GC-14-105.rationale: The storm passage (mainsail split, English screaming, Germans singing on, 'our women and children are not afraid to die') is quoted from Wesley's Journal; confirmed near-verbatim.

sources.title: John Wesley Meets the Moravians (quoting Wesley's Journal)

GC-14-106.rationale: On arriving at Savannah, Wesley lodged briefly with the Moravians (Spangenberg) and admired their conduct; from his Journal.

sources.title: Chapter VI — To America and Back (Wesley Center Online)

GC-14-107.rationale: Wesley's admiring description of a Moravian service ('almost made me forget the seventeen hundred years... Paul the tent-maker, or Peter the fisherman') is quoted from his Journal.

GC-14-108.rationale: Back in England, Wesley was instructed by the Moravian Peter Boehler and moved toward assurance by faith; documented.

sources.title: John Wesley and Faith at Aldersgate - The Gospel Coalition

GC-14-109.rationale: Wesley became convinced to renounce dependence on works and trust wholly in Christ; documented in his Journal/letters.

sources.title: John Wesley and Faith at Aldersgate - The Gospel Coalition

GC-14-110.rationale: At the Aldersgate Street Moravian society (24 May 1738), Luther's Preface to the Epistle to the Romans was being read; from Wesley's Journal.

GC-14-111.rationale: 'I felt my heart strangely warmed... I did trust in Christ, Christ alone, for salvation' is quoted verbatim from Wesley's Journal (24 May 1738); one of the most-cited passages in Christian history.

GC-14-112.rationale: Wesley concluded that saving grace is a free gift; consistent with his post-Aldersgate theology and Journal.

sources.title: John Wesley and Faith at Aldersgate - The Gospel Coalition

GC-14-113.rationale: 'I look upon all the world as my parish' is a genuine, well-known Wesley statement (from a 1739 letter to James Hervey).

GC-14-114.rationale: Wesley continued a disciplined life but reframed works as the fruit rather than the ground of faith; standard account of his theology.

GC-14-115.rationale: Wesley's ministry centered on justification by faith in Christ's atonement and the Spirit's renewing work (sanctification); standard.

GC-14-116.rationale: Whitefield and the Wesleys endured scorn at Oxford and on entering ministry after their own protracted convictions; standard biography.

GC-14-117.rationale: [common_knowledge] 'Methodist' began as a derisive Oxford nickname and by 1911 named a major denomination in Britain and America.

GC-14-118.rationale: As CoE members the leaders were attached to its liturgy/forms even while pressing revival; standard.

GC-14-119.rationale: The revival preaching of Whitefield and the Wesleys produced large numbers of conversions; well documented.

GC-14-120.rationale: Wesley organized converts into the 'Methodist Connexion' intending renewal within the CoE, not a new denomination (formal separation came after his death). GC's statement of his intent is accurate.

GC-14-121.rationale: Theological claim (God 'overruled events' so reform began within the church); not testable.

GC-14-122.rationale: Because the revival preachers were CoE clergy working inside the church, the movement gained entry and roused some parish clergy; standard historiography.

GC-14-123.rationale: Interpretive ('all moved by the Spirit of God, united in aim'); the theological attribution is not testable.

GC-14-124.rationale: The Calvinist/Arminian dispute between Whitefield and the Wesleys did threaten a breach but they were personally reconciled; well documented (Whitefield asked Wesley to preach his funeral sermon).

GC-14-125.rationale: Learned opponents attacked the Methodists and many clergy eventually closed their pulpits to them; standard history.

GC-14-126.rationale: Pulpit denunciations helped incite mob hostility against the Methodists; consistent with the record of the anti-Methodist riots.

GC-14-127.rationale: Theological claim (repeated escape by 'a miracle of God's mercy'); the providential attribution is not testable, though the underlying mob dangers were real.

GC-14-128.rationale: Theological/supernatural claim ('an angel in human form' dispersing a mob); not empirically verifiable.

GC-14-129.rationale: The mob-attack account (attempts to throw him down, the torn waistcoat flaps, the oaken stick, the man stroking his hair) is quoted from Wesley's Journal (Wednesbury/Walsall riots, Oct 1743).

GC-14-130.rationale: The tally of assaults (brick, stone between the eyes, blows to breast and mouth 'as if... a straw') is quoted from Wesley's Journal.

GC-14-131.rationale: Early Methodists (preachers and laity) suffered ridicule and persecution from churchmen and the irreligious; well documented.

GC-14-132.rationale: Methodists were sometimes hauled before magistrates; consistent with the record (e.g. Wesley's own encounters with justices).

GC-14-133.rationale: Interpretive editorial ('courts of justice—such only in name'); an evaluative aside, not a checkable fact.

GC-14-134.rationale: Anti-Methodist mobs did ransack homes, plunder goods and assault people; documented in the riots of the 1740s.

GC-14-135.rationale: Public handbills summoning people to attack Methodists are reported in Wesley's Journal and contemporary accounts; plausible and attested for the anti-Methodist violence.

GC-14-136.rationale: Interpretive/evaluative summary ('systematic persecution... whose only fault...'); a value judgment, not a discrete fact.

GC-14-137.rationale: The defense against charges of novelty/Quakerism/popery is quoted from Wesley's works (his apologetic writings); faithfully transmitted period quotation.

GC-14-138.rationale: The 'too strict / too narrow a way' passage with its Scripture texts is from Wesley's works; faithfully transmitted.

GC-14-139.rationale: The continuation on the 'steward of the mysteries of God' and the charge of 'uncharitableness' is from Wesley's works; faithfully transmitted.

GC-14-140.rationale: Wesley attributed pre-revival spiritual decline substantially to Antinomian teaching; this is his own diagnosis, accurately reported (a partisan reading, but genuinely his).

GC-14-141.rationale: Antinomians did teach that Christ abolished the moral law's obligation for believers ('freed from the bondage of good works'); an accurate summary of the historical Antinomian position Wesley opposed.

GC-14-142.rationale: The description (ministers need not exhort obedience; elect led irresistibly, reprobate unable to obey) is quoted from a period ecclesiastical history (Mosheim-type source used by White); faithfully transmitted as a characterization of hyper-Calvinist Antinomianism.

GC-14-143.rationale: The 'elect cannot fall... their wicked actions are not really sinful' claim is quoted from a period ecclesiastical-history source (Mosheim). Modern scholars regard this as a hostile caricature of extreme Antinomianism, but White transmits it faithfully.

sources.title: Mosheim, Ecclesiastical History (period characterization of Antinomians)

GC-14-144.rationale: The 'even the vilest sin is not a sin in God's sight if committed by one of the elect' formulation is quoted from the same period source; faithfully transmitted as the extreme-Antinomian caricature.

GC-14-145.rationale: Interpretive equation of Antinomianism with later moral relativism; an argumentative construal, not a checkable fact.

GC-14-146.rationale: Theological claim (all such ideas inspired by Satan, who began in heaven); not empirically testable.

GC-14-147.rationale: Wesley (an Arminian) argued that hyper-Calvinist 'divine decrees' fixing character led many toward practical rejection of the moral law; accurately his position.

GC-14-148.rationale: Wesley steadfastly opposed Antinomianism and argued its underlying predestinarianism was unscriptural; well documented (his sermons and the Calvinist controversy).

GC-14-149.rationale: Wesley cited Titus 2:11 and 1 Timothy 2:3-6 for universal grace against limited election; the quoted texts are accurate Scripture and characteristic of his Arminian argument.

sources.title: John Wesley, Works / sermons on free grace

GC-14-150.rationale: Wesley cited John 1:9 ('the true Light... lighteth every man') in support of universal grace; accurate Scripture and characteristic argument.

GC-14-151.rationale: The passage ('The moral law... He did not take away... written on the hearts of all') is from Wesley's Sermon 25 (Upon Our Lord's Sermon on the Mount, Discourse V); faithfully transmitted.

GC-14-152.rationale: Wesley's exposition of 'I am not come to destroy, but to fulfil' (to establish the law in its fullness) is from the same sermon discourse; faithfully transmitted.

GC-14-153.rationale: Wesley's teaching on the harmony of law and gospel (law points to gospel; gospel fulfils the law in us) is from his sermons; faithfully transmitted.

sources.title: John Wesley, Sermon on the Law Established Through Faith

GC-14-154.rationale: The passage placing those who 'speak evil of the law' among 'the highest rank of the enemies of the gospel' and comparing them to Judas is from Wesley's sermon on the Law; faithfully transmitted.

sources.title: John Wesley, Sermon on the Law Established Through Faith

GC-14-155.rationale: Wesley's rejection of 'the preaching of the gospel answers all the ends of the law' (the law must first convince of sin; Rom 3:20; the 'physician' saying) is from his sermon on the Law; faithfully transmitted.

sources.title: John Wesley, Sermon on the Law Established Through Faith

GC-14-156.rationale: At Wesley's death (1791) documented Methodist SOCIETY MEMBERS numbered roughly 135,000 (Britain plus America). GC's 'avowed adherents... more than half a million' far exceeds membership; it is defensible only if 'adherents' means the broader body of hearers/attenders, an estimate at the high end and not securely documented. The half-million figure descends from older Methodist historiography. Genuine ambiguity between 'members' and 'adherents' places this in dispute rather than plain error. Not load-bearing.
GC-14-156.modern_consensus: ~135,000 members at Wesley's death; ~500,000 'adherents' is an upper-bound estimate for hearers, not membership.

sources.title: Methodism - Wikipedia
sources.note: membership figures well below half a million

GC-14-157.rationale: Interpretive/homiletic ('a lesson of priceless worth to every Christian'); an evaluative statement, not a checkable fact.
